summary: O'Neil recounts a dream about Archie Bunker being elected president, drawing an implicit parallel to the 2016 election cycle. She explores the paradox that Norman Lear created Archie Bunker to be disliked, yet audiences embraced him as lovable and relatable. The post examines how views considered 'laughably old-fashioned' in the 1970s have resurfaced in mainstream politics. O'Neil questions whether the 'Archie Bunker for president' concept was ever truly a parody, suggesting it reflected genuine political sentiment all along.
key_points: Norman Lear intended Archie Bunker to be strongly disliked, but audiences loved him instead—he was voted TV's #1 character in 2005
Bunker's bigotry was excused because he was portrayed as hardworking, loving, and 'basically decent' with views that were 'merely a product' of his environment
There was an actual 'Archie Bunker voting bloc' in 1972 and a parody presidential campaign, suggesting the archetype has real political power
The post implicitly connects this 1970s character to the rise of Trump in 2016, questioning how much has really changed
money_quotes: He had brought along all of his racist, misogynistic, antisemitic, and homophobic thoughts and mannerisms.
The opinions expressed by the title character were laughably old-fashioned *at the time*.
Show creator Norman Lear originally intended that Bunker be strongly disliked by audiences. It didn't work.

Rather than being motivated by malice, he is portrayed as hardworking, a loving father and husband, as well as a basically decent man whose views are merely a product of the era and working-class environment in which he has been raised.
I guess my question is, how much of a parody is this really?
As a child, I didn't get why people liked Archie Bunker.
key_insight: The 'lovable bigot' archetype that was supposed to be satirical has always reflected a real and powerful political constituency that mainstream culture repeatedly underestimates.
